chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Computer Organization. (3-2). Credit 4. Computer systems from programmer's
perspective including simple logic design, data representation and processor
architecture, programming of processors, memory, control flow, input/output, and
performance measurements; hands-on lab assignments. Prerequisite: Grade of C
or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
Topics covered in the course are:
• Computer System
• Binary Representation
• Boolean Logic Design
• Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
• Sequential Design
• Machine Language
• CPU Architecture (Processor)
• Memory
• Virtual Memory
• Assembler
• Advanced Topics (based on time availability)

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent
enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of the course, students will be able to:
• Perform Basic Logic Design
• Understand the design of a simple computer system
• Understand elements that make up a CPU (Central Processing Unit)
• Design software with an understanding of how it will run on computers
• Participate in discussions that involve impact of hardware and software
elements on each other and the performance of the program
• Take intermediate and advanced classes in computer architecture, systems,
operating systems, cloud, parallel computing

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:

ISBN: 9780262539807

Publisher: National Geographic Books

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
grade =
if (total score >= 90) then A
else if (total score >= 80) then B
else if (total score >= 70) then C
else if (total score >= 60) then D
else F

chunks.title: Exam and Assignment Weighting
chunks.content:
Midterm 1: 15%
Midterm 2: 15%
Midterm 3: 10%
Final Exam: 30%
Lab exercises: 25%
Lab attendance: 5%
Total: 100%
(extra credits) Thoughtful and constructive participation in class discussions at instructor
discretion: max of 3%

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy
No late submissions for assignments (i.e. lab exercises) will be allowed without an
excused medical or family event reason. To receive approval for a medical or serious
family event, you must send email to the TA, with necessary documentation, such as a
Dr's note or event note, no later than 3 days from assignment due date. You must receive
approval form the TA. In case you do not receive a response from the TA within 2
business days, send me a reminder and cc the Instructor. An approval must be in place
for full credit relating to the missed assignment. For circumstances other than the above,
contact the instructor for approval.

For late submissions without excused extension the following reduction will be applied:
• 80% of score received on assignment for late submission no greater than 3 days
from deadline
• 60% of score received on assignment for late submission more than 3 days but no
greater than 1 week from deadline
• 40% of score received on assignment for late submission more than 1 week but no
greater than 2 weeks from deadline
• 0% otherwise

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
*subject to changes based on weather, unanticipated conditions,
instructor illness/unavailability, class dynamics

Week 1 (Jan 12–16)
• Course logistics & introduction
• Boolean logic fundamentals

Week 2 (Jan 19–23)
• Decimal → Binary conversion
• Logic expressions using AND/OR/NOT

Week 2 (Jan 19–23)
• MLK Holiday (Jan 19)
• Truth tables & Boolean algebra
• Logic gates

Week 3 (Jan 26-30)
• Combinational logic
• NAND-based designs
• Boolean minimization techniques

Week 4 (Feb 2–6)
• Binary addition & subtraction
• Multiplexers and demultiplexers
• Intro to sequential logic & memory

Week 5 (Feb 9–13)
• Modular memory
• State machines
• Exam 1

Week 6 (Feb 16–20)
• State machines continued
• Machine language concepts

Week 7 (Feb 23–27)
• HACK machine language
• Writing basic HACK programs

Week 8 (Mar 2–6)
• Building a small CPU
• Exam 2

Week 9 (Mar 9–13)
• Spring Break

Week 10 (Mar 16–20)
• HACK architecture
• HACK CPU
• Assembly programming

Week 11 (Mar 23-27)
• Modern CPU concepts
• Memory hierarchy

Week 12 (Mar 30-Apr 3)
• Virtual memory
• Caches

Week 13 (Apr 6–10)
• Advanced caching
• Superscalar processors
• Exam 3

Week 14 (Apr 13–17)
• Pipelining
• CPU optimization techniques

Week 15 (Apr 20–24)
• Assembler design
• CPU design problems

Final Week (Apr 27-28)
• Wrap-up / buffer
• Last day of class Apr 28
• Final Exam May 1st 10:30 am - 12:30 pm in Lecture Room

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
• You are not allowed to use any Al tool for any assignment, lab work, exams or
reports.
• All detected plagiarism, cheating, and complicity will be reported to the Aggie
Honor System Office.
• The sanction is a zero on the assignment and a letter grade reduction for a single
infraction.
• The sanction can be a letter grade For F* for a pattern of infractions.
• Academic dishonesty will not be tolerated. For assignments, such as status reports
or poster creation, the group is expected to write their material. Do not use Al tools!
Not even for polishing your report or correcting grammar. Use spellcheckers and
grammar checkers in document editors.
• Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any portion of any
assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write
assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT, Gemini.
• Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to help
another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also constitutes academic
dishonesty and carries the same punishment as cheating and plagiarism.
• Example if you have direct knowledge that another person on the class is using Al
tools for unapproved work, and you do not report it immediately, you will be subject
to the same sanction as the individual committing plagiarism or unauthorized
access of Al tools.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Attendance is mandatory for all lectures and laboratories except for the following
situations:
• For medical reasons, which requires documentation and an email to the TA (for
Lab) or to the Instructor (for Lectures)
• For immediate family health reasons. Also requires documentation and email to TA
or Instructor.
• 2 unexcused absences are allowed for lectures and for labs each (ie a total of 4).

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
If you miss a mid semester exam, you will have to schedule a separate exam with the TA
within 1 week of the missed exam. Note that only documented medical or family
situations can be reasons for reschedule.
If you miss the final exam, contact the instructor immediately.
If you have 3 exams on May 1st (finals) contact the instructor for alternate times no later
than 10 days prior to the final exam.
